text.title: PLG Core Metric Catalog
text.summary: Core PLG metrics with formulas, benchmarks, ownership rules, and the anti-pattern of NRR-only tracking.

rule.statement: Assign one owner per metric. Define the activation event as ONE event, in ONE table, fired in ONE place.
rule.rationale: Without an owner, metric definitions drift and the dashboard becomes folklore. Cross-source UNION aggregations for activation always break when schemas evolve. A single-source activation event is the prerequisite for reliable funnel analysis.

rule.statement: Keep the metric catalog version-controlled (dbt + Git). Pair every dashboard tile with a runbook line: "if this metric moves, who acts and how."
rule.rationale: Dashboards that change metric definitions silently are net-negative — they create false confidence. Without a runbook, teams review the metric but take no action, which is the same as not measuring it.

list.item: Sign-up to Activation: activated / signups — benchmark 40-60%.
Free to Paid Conversion: paid / free users — benchmark 2-5% (freemium), 10-25% (trial).
Time to Value (TTV): median time from signup to aha moment — target under 5 minutes.
Natural Rate of Growth: (organic + viral) / total signups — target above 50%.
PQL to Customer: customers / PQLs — benchmark 15-30%.
Expansion MRR: expansion MRR / total MRR — target above 30%.
Net Revenue Retention: (MRR + expansion - churn) / MRR — target above 120%.

rule.statement: Always track both NRR and gross dollar retention (GDR). Never report NRR alone.
rule.rationale: NRR above 100% can conceal logo churn: expansion revenue from remaining accounts masks a shrinking customer base. GDR reveals the erosion that NRR hides.

rule.statement: Track TTV segmented by cohort, acquisition source, user segment (SMB vs Enterprise), and onboarding path — never as a single global median.
rule.rationale: Global TTV median hides large variance between segments. An Enterprise-heavy cohort with 30-minute TTV and an SMB cohort with 2-minute TTV produce a misleading median that drives wrong optimization decisions.
